title: Ceradenia comorensis
what_description: Ceradenia comorensis is a species of epiphytic fern in the family Polypodiaceae. It has a rhizome that produces pinnate fronds, with linear-lanceolate pinnae and a rachis that is densely covered with dark-brown scales. The plant is native to the Comoros Islands and has been found growing on tree trunks and branches. The fronds of Ceradenia comorensis are typically 20-50 cm long and have a lobe-like shape, with the lower pinnae being smaller than the upper ones. The plant's sporophylls are dimorphic, with the sterile sporophylls being larger and more robust than the fertile ones.

The plant's antimicrobial properties have been found to inhibit the growth of various microorganisms, including bacteria and fungi, making it a potential candidate for the treatment of infections. preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Ceradenia comorensis are used for medicinal purposes. Tea is made by infusing the leaves in hot water to treat digestive issues and reduce inflammation. Tincture is prepared by soaking the plant in a solvent, often ethanol, to create a concentrated liquid extract for pain relief and antimicrobial properties. Infusion involves steeping the leaves in hot water to make a decoction that treats fever and coughs.
